blocks.body: Reported Questions explains how to report yes/no questions and wh-questions without keeping direct-question inversion.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Use reported questions when you retell a question as part of another sentence instead of asking it directly.","rules":[{"label":"Yes / no questions","color":"emerald","text":"For general questions we most often use if or whether.","example":"She asked if I was ready."},{"label":"Wh-questions","color":"blue","text":"For special questions we keep the question word: where, why, when, how, and so on.","example":"He asked where I lived."},{"label":"No inversion","color":"amber","text":"In the reported clause there is no question-style inversion. The word order becomes declarative, and a question mark is usually not needed.","example":"She asked what time the film started."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Form and structure
blocks.body:
For reported questions we often use the pattern subject + asked + reported clause.

  • Yes / no question → if / whether + statement order: He asked if I was free.
  • Wh-question → question word + statement order: She asked where I lived.
  • After the change to reported speech, we do not keep inversion such as where did you live; the correct form is where I lived.
  • Inside a larger sentence, the question mark usually disappears.

blocks.heading: 2. Main uses and examples
blocks.body:
Direct questionReported questionComment
“Do you like coffee?”She asked if I liked coffee.Yes / no question → if
“Are they coming tonight?”He asked whether they were coming that night.whether works too
“Where do you live?”She asked where I lived.where stays, inversion disappears
“Why did you leave early?”They asked why I had left early.question word + backshift

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: keeping the auxiliary before the subject.
    Better: He asked where I lived, not He asked where did I live.
  • Mistake: keeping a question mark inside the reported clause.
    Better: She asked if I was ready.
  • Mistake: forgetting if / whether in yes/no questions.
    Better: They asked whether we needed help.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Yes / no questions become if / whether + statement order.
  • Wh-questions keep the question word, but lose inversion.
  • A reported question passes on the meaning of the question, so the grammar becomes closer to a statement.
